title: Lecture 3 Swaps 2024T1
body:
Lecture 3 – More Hedging with Futures and Introduction to Swaps

Hull Chapter 3 & 7  Hedging market risk with stock index futures

• A stock index is a broad portfolio of stocks • ASX 300 index • S&P 500 index • MSCI World index

• The CAPM makes the distinction between systematic and idiosyncratic risk:

• We want to hedge the systematic component of returns • Specifically, we imagine that we have invested some amount, 𝑉𝑆 , in a stock and we want to neutralize the market risk. • The stock is positively exposed to market risk. • Reducing market risk means shorting stock futures index. • How many futures contracts, N*, should we short?

Hedging market risk

• Case 1:

• Suppose that the maturities of our exposure and the stock index futures are equal and thus match

• Moreover, suppose that the asset we want to hedge has a β = 1

• On average, 1% return of the index would result in a 1% return for the asset.

• Because +/-1% in the index translates into +/-1% in the stock, we would ∗ short 𝑁 = 1 ∙ 𝑉𝑆 /𝑉𝐹 contracts

Hedging market risk

Case 2: • Suppose that the maturities of our exposure and the stock index futures are the same but that the portfolio we want to hedge has a β ≠ 1.

• On average, a 1% excess return on the index results in a β% excess return on the asset

• We need to scale our hedge by β • In other words: ℎ∗ = 𝛽 • In fact, we can show that

∗ • We short 𝑁 = 𝛽 ∙ 𝑉𝑆 /𝑉𝐹 contracts  Rolling a hedge forward

• We have focused on mismatched maturities where the maturity of the futures contract is later than the maturity of our exposure.

• But what to do if we are in the opposite situation? What if our exposure is later than the maturity of tradable futures (i.e. where 𝑇 < 𝑡2 )?

• When such a situation occurs, we hedge our exposure up until time T using a given available contract. Then, we will need to roll our hedge.

• Once we arrive at time T, we roll the hedge forward by entering a new contract that matures at time 𝑡2 (or later).

• What is the cash flow when we roll our hedge?

• As the relative futures prices at time T are stochastic, we would face an additional risk source 5  Swaps

• A swap is an agreements to trade (swap) one cash flow stream for another.

• A forward contract can be seen as a swap in which the cash flow from the sale of an asset is swapped for a fixed cash flow. A swap can be seen as a portfolio of forwards.

• Typically swaps involve more than one exchange of cash flows, e.g. each six month until contract maturity.

• The cash-flow streams can depend on a range of variables, such as: • Fixed and floating interest rates • Exchange rates • Commodity prices • Equity returns • Credit defaults • Return volatilities  Interest Rate Swaps: The LIBOR

• Many interest rate swaps that we will deal with depend on the London Interbank Offer Rate, or the LIBOR for short.

• LIBOR spot-rates are available with maturities up to one year

• A “plain vanilla” interest rate swap exchanges the cash flows from a floating rate bond for the cash flows from a fixed rate bond • The floating rate is typically defined in terms of a reference rate such as the LIBOR, e.g. LIBOR + 1% • The rate used to calculate the cash flows of a period is typically the floating rate at the start of the period, and paid at the end of the period • Both bonds have the same principal (or face value), L, so that payment nets out

Interest rate swaps

• A “plain vanilla” interest rate swap exchanges the cash flows from a floating rate bond for the cash flows from a fixed rate bond • The floating rate is typically defined in terms of a reference rate such as the LIBOR, e.g. LIBOR + 1% • The rate used to calculate the cash flows of a period (in our examples a year) is typically the floating rate at the start of the period , and paid at end of the period • Both bonds have the same principal (or face value), L, so that payment nets out

• For simplicity, we’ll use annually compounded interest rates when calculating cash flows in interest rate swaps

• We’ll still use continuously compounded rates to calculate the present value of cash flows (discounting rate)  Example: Interest rate swaps

• Consider a 3-year swap between companies A and B. A agrees to pay B an interest rate of 5% per annum on a principal of $100 million and B agrees to pay A the 6month LIBOR rate on the same principal. The payments will be exchanged every six months. • A will pay B the fixed rate of 5%, • B will pay A the floating rate of LIBOR • The notional principal is $100 million • Cash flows are exchanged annually for the next three years • The cash flows at time t, see Table 7.1 and Table 7.2

Example: Interest rate swaps The cash flows at time t, see Table 7.1 and Table 7.2

Pricing swaps

• Much like with forward contracts, the parameters of swap contracts are chosen so as to set the value of the swap equal to zero (or at least close to zero) when it is first initiated. • As time passes, the reference (LIBOR) rate will change, generally making the value of the swap, V, non-zero. • A swap is a zero-sum game: If the value is V to one party, then it is – 𝑉 to the other (just like short v.s. long forward positions). • To figure out the price of a swap agreement it is useful to think of it as a position in two bonds.

Pricing swaps

• The value of the swap to the party receiving the floating rate payments (A in last example) is:

• The pricing issue is reduced to finding 𝐵𝑓𝑙 and 𝐵𝑓𝑖𝑥 .

Calculating 𝑩𝒇𝒊𝒙 : • We will use the following facts • The price of a bond is the sum of the present value of its cash flows. • Banks use the LIBOR as a proxy for the risk-free for pricing purposes. • Since the cash flows of the fixed-rate bond are known at time 0, pricing it is simply a matter of summing up the present values:

• Note that: • The appropriate discount rate for time t is the LIBOR zero rate for time t • The cash flow at time T includes the notional principal L 12  Pricing swaps

Calculating 𝑩𝒇𝒍 :

• A floating-rate bond is worth the notional principal (L) right after an coupon (interest) payment.

• At the first payment date t*, 𝐵𝑓𝑙 = 𝐿 + 𝐶𝑡∗ as we will receive the 𝐶𝑡∗ coupon (interest) payment.

• The value of the floating-rate bond at time t is simply 𝑃𝑉(𝐿 + 𝐶𝑡∗ ) discounted from t* to t. • Note that 𝐶𝑡∗ is known at time t, since it is based on previous period LIBOR. • We get the present value by discounting the cash flow with the appropriate LIBOR zero −𝑟 ∗ (𝑡 ∗ −𝑡) rate: 𝑒 .

Pricing an interest rate swap – 7.3

• A $100 million interest rate swap has remaining life of 10 months. Under the terms of the swap, 6-month LIBOR is exchanges for 7% per annum (compounded semiannually). The average of the bid-offer rate being exchanged for 6-month LIBOR in swaps of all maturities is currently 5% per annum with continuous compounding. The 6-month LIBOR rate was 4.6% per annum 2 months ago. Wha is the current value of the swap to the party paying floating rate?

Step 1: figure out timeline for payment dates Step 2: figure out payments for both fixed rate and floating rate bonds Step 3: calculate the present value of the cash flows for both bonds. Step 4: Find the value of the swap as the difference between the bond values

Currency swaps

• Currency swaps are similar to interest rate swaps, except that the principal earns interest in different currencies

• Since exchange rates move, the principal of the two bonds may not be worth the same at maturity

• The principal is exchanged at the beginning and at the end of the contract

• One or both interest rates can be fixed (and the others floating)

• From the perspective of the domestic/local country, the value of the foreign denominated cash flows vary with the exchange rate, even if they are calculated using fixed rate • There is a clear parallel to plain vanilla interest rate swaps

• We’ll use annually compounded interest rates for the agreed cash flows, although we could easily express the same calculations using continuous rates  Example: Currency swaps

• Suppose companies A and B enters a swap agreement with the following characteristics: • A will pay B a fixed rate of 10% in Euros • B will pay A a fixed rate of 12% in Australian dollars • Cash flows are exchanged annually, i.e. one period = one year • The notional principals are 80 Euro and 100 AUD • S is the number of AUD per unit of Euro

• A possible outcome for B would be (in AUD):

Pricing currency swaps

• We’ll denote the value of a swap agreement by V • As time passes, exchange rates will change, and so will 𝑉𝑡 • Ignoring transaction costs, a swap is a zero-sum game: If the value is V to one party, it is –V to the other

• To figure out the price of a swap agreement it is useful to think of it as consisting of two bonds.

• In a currency swap the principal is usually exchanged at the beginning and the end of the swap’s life

• Since the cash flows of fixed-rate bonds are known at time 0, pricing them (in the local currency) is simply a matter of summing up the present values: 17  Pricing currency swaps

• Suppose we have just entered a swap with the following conditions: • We receive a fixed rate, 𝑟𝐹 , on a foreign denominated principal 𝐿𝐹 each year • We pay a fixed rate, 𝑟𝐷 , on a domestic denominated principal 𝐿𝐷 each year • The life of the swap agreement is two years • S is the number of domestic currency to get 1unit of foreign. • We can ignore the initial exchange of the principals because it offsets by construction.

• As before we value the swap agreement by separately valuing the long and the short bonds and taking the difference. The value to the local investor is

• We will have to convert the foreign value to the domestic currency at the current spot exchange rate.

Pricing currency swaps

• Pricing the domestic bond is trivial – just discount the cash-flows • We typically use the LIBOR rate as a discount rate

• Pricing the foreign bond is almost the same, but note that the correct zerorates used to discount future cash flows are the foreign (LIBOR) rates

• Since the bonds are the total present values of future cash flows, we can use the current spot rate to get the foreign bond’s domestic value

Pricing currency swaps : 7.5

• A currency swap has a remaining life of 15 months. It involves exchanging interest at 10% on £ 20 million for interest at 6% on $30 million once a year. The term structure of interest rates in both the United Kingdom and the United States is currently flat, and if the swap were negotiated today the interest rates exchanged would be 4% in dollars and 7% in sterling. All interest rates are quoted with annual compounding. The current exchange rate (dollars per pound sterling) is 1.8500. What is the value of the swap to the party sterling?

• Step 1: figure out the timeline for exchanging the interest payments • Step 2: calculate the interest payments in both currencies (10% in sterling and 6% in dollars) • Step 3 : calculate the present value of cash flows for each currency (4% discount rate in dollars and 7% in sterling) • Step 4: Find the value of the swap (convert foreign currency to local currency)

Financial intermediaries

• Until now, we have talked about swaps as a direct agreement between two parties • In reality, some financial institution will act as a middleman • These intermediaries take on certain risks • The intermediary tries to hedge its risk by entering into opposite sides in two similar swap agreements • When this is not possible, they will seek to hedge the risk in other ways • The loss in a swap agreement is typically offset by the gain in the other • However, there is always a risk that the party on which the financial intermediary has a claim defaults • Since principals are nominal, this is not as bad as when a bond issuer defaults (but still bad) • To compensate for these risks the financial intermediaries will charge a spread

Financial intermediaries

5% 5.2% Intel MSFT

LIBOR+0.1% LIBOR

4.985% 5.015%

5.2% Intel F. I. MSFT

LIBOR+0.1% LIBOR LIBOR

Exercise questions

• Valuation of Interest Rate Swaps: You do not have to study the Valuation in Terms of FRAs (p 162-163 of the book).

• Chapter 3: 3.18 – 3.23
